name: Phoebus Cartel
description: The Phoebus Cartel refers to a secretive alleged agreement among major light bulb manufacturers in the 1920s to intentionally limit the lifespan of incandescent light bulbs. The theory posits that this was done to increase sales and profits by forcing consumers to replace bulbs more frequently. Proponents claim that evidence for this cartel exists in historical documents and the observable lifespan of light bulbs produced before and after the alleged agreement. It's argued that pre-Phoebus light bulbs lasted significantly longer than those produced after the cartel's formation. The mainstream perspective acknowledges the existence of an agreement among manufacturers but frames it as an attempt to standardize bulb quality and efficiency, not necessarily to deliberately shorten lifespan. Historians point to the complexities of early 20th-century manufacturing and the challenges of producing consistently long-lasting bulbs. They argue that variations in lifespan were likely due to technological limitations and inconsistent manufacturing processes rather than a deliberate conspiracy. Furthermore, critics highlight the difficulty of definitively proving intent to deceive consumers, as well as the limitations of comparing bulb lifespans across different eras with varying technological standards and usage patterns. The theory remains popular in conspiracy circles, often cited as an example of planned obsolescence and corporate greed.
subtitle: Planned obsolescence in light bulbs?

claims.claim: The Phoebus Cartel intentionally shortened light bulb lifespans.
claims.evidence: Historical documents referencing agreements between light bulb manufacturers to standardize lifespan around 1000 hours.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians argue the agreement aimed to standardize bulb quality and efficiency, not necessarily to deliberately shorten lifespan. Variations in early bulb lifespan were common due to technological limitations.

claims.claim: Light bulbs produced before the Phoebus Cartel lasted significantly longer.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal evidence and claims of pre-1920s bulbs lasting for many years.
claims.criticism: Lack of reliable data and controlled experiments to compare lifespan across different eras. Technological advancements and changes in usage patterns make direct comparisons difficult.

claims.claim: The Phoebus Cartel demonstrates the power of corporate greed and planned obsolescence.
claims.evidence: The alleged agreement highlights the potential for companies to manipulate product lifecycles for profit.
claims.criticism: While planned obsolescence exists, the Phoebus Cartel's impact is debated. Modern light bulb technology focuses on energy efficiency and different lighting technologies like LEDs have different lifespan characteristics.

keyFigures.name: Anton Philips
keyFigures.role: Head of Philips during the Phoebus Cartel era.

keyFigures.name: Mark Kaufman
keyFigures.role: Author critical of the Phoebus Cartel theory, highlighting technological factors.

timeline.event: Alleged formation of the Phoebus Cartel.
Cartel officially dissolved due to World War II.

sources.author: Mark Kaufman
sources.work: The Consumer Trap: Big Business Marketing in American Life
sources.title: Mark Kaufman
